Multivariate Statistics Project

Nicholas Nason  ·  Stonehill College  ·  Spring 2026

BRFSS 2024 SAS Viya Directed Study n = 25,000
Project Goal About the Data Data Prep t-Test Factor Analysis Regression ANOVA Logistic Regression Conclusion

Project Goal: Utilizing survey results from The Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) to examine whether behavioral, demographic, and social determinant factors are associated with poor physical and mental health outcomes among U.S. Adults, and to evaluate whether these factors can predict an individual's risk of chronic diseases.

About the Data: The Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) is an annual telephone survey conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that collects data on health behaviors, chronic conditions, and preventive health practices from over 400,000 U.S. adults each year. This project uses a random sample of 25,000 BRFSS 2024 respondents to apply the multivariate statistical methods covered over the course of this directed study to a real-world public health dataset. The central research question is: what behavioral, demographic, and social determinant factors are associated with poor physical and mental health outcomes among U.S. adults, and can those factors be used to predict and classify chronic disease risk? This framing allows each statistical technique to contribute a distinct and meaningful angle to a single unified investigation rather than treating each method as an isolated exercise. Six analytical techniques are applied across six phases. The work begins with hypothesis testing to establish baseline relationships in the data, moves through factor analysis to uncover latent structure among social determinant variables, applies multiple linear regression and ANOVA/MANOVA to model and compare group differences in health outcomes, uses logistic regression to predict diabetes risk, and optionally applies discriminant analysis to classify general health status. Each analysis section follows a consistent write-up format: objective, variables, hypotheses, statistical procedure, results, interpretation, and diagnostic checks. The SAS code for all analyses is contained in the companion file BRFSS_Analysis_Code.sas and should be run in SAS Viya Workbench. All result placeholders shown in italicized brackets should be replaced with actual output after running the code.

SAS Code
/*An Over of the contents of our dataset as it is a simple and not the entire data set from the BRFSS*/
 /*This will produce a  data dictionary, dispalying: variable names, types, lengths, labels*/
 
proc contents data = SASUSER.BRFSS varnum;
run;

283 LCSLAST_ Num 8 LCSLAST_. HOW OLD WHEN YOU LAST SMOKED?

287 _PACKDAY Num 8   NUMBER OF PACKS OF CIGARETTES SMOKED PER
288 _PACKYRS Num 8   YEARS SMOKED REPORTED PACKS PER DAY
289 _LCSYQTS Num 8   NUMBER OF YEARS SINCE QUIT SMOKING CIGAR

291 _LCSELIG Num 8 _LCSELIG. USPSTF ELIGIBILITY FOR SCREENING
292 _LCSCTSN Num 8 _LCSCTSN. HAD CHEST CT SCAN FOR LUNG CANCER
293 _LCSPSTF Num 8 _LCSPSTF. MEET USPSTF LUNG CANCER SCREENING GUIDEL
294 DRNKANY6 Num 8 DRNK6ANY. DRINK ANY ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES IN PAST 30
295 DROCDY4_ Num 8 DROCDY4_. COMPUTED DRINK-OCCASIONS-PER-DAY
296 _RFBING6 Num 8 _6RFBING. BINGE DRINKING CALCULATED VARIABLE
297 _DRNKWK3 Num 8 _DRNK3WK. COMPUTED NUMBER OF DRINKS OF ALCOHOL BEV
298 _RFDRHV9 Num 8 _9RFDRHV. HEAVY ALCOHOL CONSUMPTION CALCULATED VA
299 _FLSHOT7 Num 8 _7FLSHOT. FLU SHOT CALCULATED VARIABLE
300 _PNEUMO3 Num 8 _3PNEUMO. PNEUMONIA VACCINATION CALCULATED VARIABL
301 _AIDTST4 Num 8 _4AIDTST. EVER BEEN TESTED FOR HIV CALCULATED VARI
302 ADLTCHLD Num 8 ADLTCHLD.  
303 AGE Num 8 AGE.  
304 CALLBACK Num 8 CALLBACK.  
305 CHILDAGE Num 8 CHILDAGE.  
306 LCSLAST Num 8 LCSLAST.  
307 PRECALL Num 8 PRECALL.  
308 RCSBORG1 Num 8 RCS1BORG.  
309 RSPSTAT1 Num 8 RSP1STAT.  
310 SECSCRFL Num 8 SECSCRFL.

312 _IMPAGE Num 8 _IMPAGE.  
313 _IMPCAGE Num 8 _IMPCAGE.  
314 _IMPCRAC Num 8 _IMPCRAC.  
315 _IMPCSEX Num 8 _IMPCSEX.  
316 _IMPEDUC Num 8 _IMPEDUC.  
317 _IMPHOME Num 8 _IMPHOME.  
318 _IMPMRTL Num 8 _IMPMRTL.

320 _IMPSEX Num 8 _IMPSEX.  
321 _M_RACE Num 8 _M_RACE.  
322 _URBNRRL Num 8 _URBNRRL.  
SAS Code
proc print data=SASUSER.BRFSS (obs=10);
run;
SAS Code
/*An Over of the contents of our dataset as it is a simple and not the entire data set from the BRFSS*/

/*Produces Summary Stats for the numeric variables in the dataset (count, mean, min, max etc.)*/
proc means data = SASUSER.BRFSS n nmiss mean std min max;
run;

SAS Code
/*An Over of the contents of our dataset as it is a simple and not the entire data set from the BRFSS*/

/*Produces frequency counts for the categorical variables in the dataset*/
proc freq data = SASUSER.BRFSS;
run;

With the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (Claude), data preparation was completed to ensure all variables were correctly coded prior to analysis:

Before any statistical analysis can be conducted, the raw BRFSS variables require recoding to address the survey's non-standard coding conventions. Several variables use special numeric codes to represent zero-day responses or don't-know and refused answers, and these must be handled explicitly so they are not treated as valid numeric values. PHYSHLTH and MENTHLTH use the value 88 to represent zero poor health days. Without recoding, 88 would be interpreted as 88 days of poor health and severely distort all means, regression coefficients, and group comparisons. Both variables are recoded so that 88 becomes 0, and values of 77 (don't know) and 99 (refused) are set to missing. The diabetes variable DIABETE4 has six response categories in this dataset, including yes during pregnancy only and pre-diabetes. For logistic regression, which requires a binary outcome, it is recoded to 1 = diabetic and 0 = not diabetic. All other response categories are set to missing, since the research question concerns clinical diabetes diagnosis specifically. BMI is stored in BRFSS as the true BMI multiplied by 100 in the variable _BMI5. Dividing by 100 restores the interpretable value. The categorical version _BMI5CAT is also retained and converted to a character label for use as a grouping factor in the ANOVA. Several binary behavioral and demographic predictors are recoded for consistency. EXERANY2 (exercise), SMOKE100 (smoking), and ADDEPEV3 (depression diagnosis) all use 2 = No in the raw data rather than 0 = No, and are recoded to standard 0/1 dummy variables. SEXVAR is recoded from 1 = Male / 2 = Female to 0 = Male / 1 = Female. For income (INCOME3), don't-know responses (77) and refused responses (99) are set to missing alongside pre-existing missing values. For education (EDUCA), the refused code (9) is set to missing. The social determinant variables fall into two groups. Four are frequency-scale variables -- LSATISFY (life satisfaction, 1-4), EMTSUPRT (emotional support, 1-5), SDLONELY (loneliness, 1-5), and SDHFOOD1 (food insecurity, 1-5) -- where don't-know and refused responses are set to missing. Note that LSATISFY uses only codes 7 and 9 as invalid codes; no code 8 exists for this variable. Two are binary Yes/No questions: SDHBILLS (unable to pay bills) and SDHTRNSP (transportation barrier), both recoded to 1 = Yes and 0 = No with don't-know and refused responses set to missing. Three additional chronic condition variables were added to support the factor analysis in Phase 3. CVDINFR4 (heart attack diagnosis), HAVARTH4 (arthritis diagnosis), and CHCCOPD3 (COPD or emphysema diagnosis) are each standard Yes/No questions in the BRFSS. All three use 1 = Yes and 2 = No in the raw data, with 7 (don't know) and 9 (refused) as their only invalid codes. Each was recoded to 1 = Yes and 0 = No for consistency with the other binary variables in the dataset. Counts were confirmed against the exploratory output: heart attack (Yes = 1,477), arthritis (Yes = 8,705), and COPD (Yes = 2,093). One important limitation identified during exploratory analysis is that the raw AGE variable is fully missing across all 25,000 observations. Age is instead captured through the BRFSS-computed variable _AGE80, which stores imputed continuous age values up to a cap of 80. This variable is renamed AGE_C and used as the age predictor in all regression and logistic models. After all recoding, proc means and proc freq were run to confirm that every variable had the expected range and reasonable non-missing counts. All recodes were validated against the output before proceeding to analysis.

SAS Code
/*Data was cleaned with the asistance of Ai (Claude)*/

data brfss_clean;
    set SASUSER.BRFSS;

    /* --- OUTCOME VARIABLES --- */

    /* PHYSHLTH: days physical health was not good in past 30 days
       Confirmed values: 1-30 (days), 88 (None = zero days),
       77 (Don't know), 99 (Refused)                              */
    if PHYSHLTH = 88 then PHYSHLTH_R = 0;
    else if PHYSHLTH in (77, 99, .) then PHYSHLTH_R = .;
    else PHYSHLTH_R = PHYSHLTH;

    /* MENTHLTH: days mental health was not good
       Same coding structure as PHYSHLTH                          */
    if MENTHLTH = 88 then MENTHLTH_R = 0;
    else if MENTHLTH in (77, 99, .) then MENTHLTH_R = .;
    else MENTHLTH_R = MENTHLTH;

    /* DIABETE4: diabetes diagnosis
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes), 2 (Pregnancy-only), 3 (No),
       4 (Pre-diabetes), 7 (Don't know), 9 (Refused)
       Keep only definitive Yes/No -- all else to missing         */
    if DIABETE4 = 1 then DIABETES_BIN = 1;
    else if DIABETE4 = 3 then DIABETES_BIN = 0;
    else DIABETES_BIN = .;

    /* GENHLTH: general health (created but not used in final analyses) */
    if GENHLTH in (1, 2) then HEALTH_BIN = 1;
    else if GENHLTH in (4, 5) then HEALTH_BIN = 0;
    else HEALTH_BIN = .;

    /* --- PREDICTOR VARIABLES --- */

    /* BMI: _BMI5 stored as BMI x 100 in BRFSS -- divide by 100  */
    if _BMI5 not in (., 9999) then BMI = _BMI5 / 100;
    else BMI = .;

    /* BMI Category (_BMI5CAT)
       Confirmed values: 1 (Underweight), 2 (Normal Weight),
       3 (Overweight), 4 (Obese), 2382 missing observations      */
    if _BMI5CAT in (1, 2, 3, 4) then BMI_CAT = _BMI5CAT;
    else BMI_CAT = .;

    /* Character label for BMI category (required for proc glm)   */
    if BMI_CAT = 1 then BMI_LABEL = 'Underweight';
    else if BMI_CAT = 2 then BMI_LABEL = 'Normal     ';
    else if BMI_CAT = 3 then BMI_LABEL = 'Overweight ';
    else if BMI_CAT = 4 then BMI_LABEL = 'Obese      ';

    /* AGE: raw AGE variable is fully missing in this dataset.
       Use _AGE80 (BRFSS imputed age, capped at 80) instead.
       Confirmed: no missing values, range 18-80.                 */
    if _AGE80 not in (., 7, 9) then AGE_C = _AGE80;
    else AGE_C = .;

    /* EXERANY2: exercised in past 30 days
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=19,141), 2 (No=5,795),
       7 (Don't know=38), 9 (Refused=26)                         */
    if EXERANY2 = 1 then EXERCISE = 1;
    else if EXERANY2 = 2 then EXERCISE = 0;
    else EXERCISE = .;

    /* SMOKE100: smoked at least 100 cigarettes in lifetime
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=9,063), 2 (No=14,282),
       7 (Don't know=113), 9 (Refused=23), 1519 pre-missing      */
    if SMOKE100 = 1 then SMOKER = 1;
    else if SMOKE100 = 2 then SMOKER = 0;
    else SMOKER = .;

    /* Character label for smoking group                          */
    if SMOKER = 1 then SMOKE_GRP = 'Smoker    ';
    else if SMOKER = 0 then SMOKE_GRP = 'Non-Smoker';

    /* ADDEPEV3: ever diagnosed with depression
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=5,266), 2 (No=19,575),
       7 (Don't know=121), 9 (Refused=38)                        */
    if ADDEPEV3 = 1 then DEPRESSED = 1;
    else if ADDEPEV3 = 2 then DEPRESSED = 0;
    else DEPRESSED = .;

    /* SEXVAR: confirmed no missing values (Male=11,906, Female=13,094)
       Recoded to 0=Male, 1=Female for regression                 */
    if SEXVAR = 1 then SEX = 0;
    else if SEXVAR = 2 then SEX = 1;
    else SEX = .;

    /* INCOME3: household income level
       NOTE: 2024 BRFSS expanded to 11 categories (1-11 ordinal).
       Confirmed max = 11 from Phase 1 output. Higher = more income.
       77 (Don't know) and 99 (Refused) set to missing.          */
    if INCOME3 in (77, 99, .) then INCOME = .;
    else INCOME = INCOME3;

    /* EDUCA: education level (1=no HS diploma to 6=college grad)
       Confirmed: code 9 (Refused=129 cases) is only invalid code */
    if EDUCA in (9, .) then EDUCATION = .;
    else EDUCATION = EDUCA;

    /* --- SOCIAL DETERMINANT VARIABLES (for Factor Analysis) --- */

    /* LSATISFY: life satisfaction (1=Very satisfied to 4=Very dissatisfied)
       Confirmed invalid codes: 7 (Don't know=67), 9 (Refused=39)
       No code 8 exists for this variable                         */
    if LSATISFY in (7, 9, .) then LSATISFY_R = .;
    else LSATISFY_R = LSATISFY;

    /* EMTSUPRT: emotional support availability (1=Always to 5=Never)
       Confirmed invalid codes: 7 (Don't know=93), 9 (Refused=38) */
    if EMTSUPRT in (7, 8, 9, .) then EMTSUPRT_R = .;
    else EMTSUPRT_R = EMTSUPRT;

    /* SDLONELY: loneliness frequency (1=Always to 5=Never)
       Confirmed invalid codes: 7 (Don't know=29), 9 (Refused=25) */
    if SDLONELY in (7, 8, 9, .) then SDLONELY_R = .;
    else SDLONELY_R = SDLONELY;

    /* SDHFOOD1: food insecurity frequency (1=Always to 5=Never)
       Confirmed invalid codes: 7 (Don't know=27), 9 (Refused=15) */
    if SDHFOOD1 in (7, 8, 9, .) then SDHFOOD1_R = .;
    else SDHFOOD1_R = SDHFOOD1;

    /* SDHBILLS: unable to pay bills (Yes/No binary question)
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=1,019), 2 (No=10,069),
       7 (Don't know=22), 9 (Refused=20), 13870 not asked/missing
       Recoded to 1=Yes (hardship), 0=No -- consistent with
       TRANSPORT_BARRIER on same 0/1 scale                        */
    if SDHBILLS = 1 then SDHBILLS_R = 1;
    else if SDHBILLS = 2 then SDHBILLS_R = 0;
    else SDHBILLS_R = .;

    /* SDHTRNSP: transportation access barrier (Yes/No question)
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=771), 2 (No=10,309),
       7 (Don't know=13), 9 (Refused=11), 13896 not asked/missing */
    if SDHTRNSP = 1 then TRANSPORT_BARRIER = 1;
    else if SDHTRNSP = 2 then TRANSPORT_BARRIER = 0;
    else TRANSPORT_BARRIER = .;

    /* --- CHRONIC CONDITION VARIABLES (for Factor Analysis) --- */

    /* CVDINFR4: ever diagnosed with a heart attack
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=1,477), 2 (No=23,354),
       7 (Don't know=161), 9 (Refused=8)                         */
    if CVDINFR4 = 1 then HEARTATTACK = 1;
    else if CVDINFR4 = 2 then HEARTATTACK = 0;
    else HEARTATTACK = .;

    /* HAVARTH4: ever told you have arthritis
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=8,705), 2 (No=16,160),
       7 (Don't know=122), 9 (Refused=13)                        */
    if HAVARTH4 = 1 then ARTHRITIS = 1;
    else if HAVARTH4 = 2 then ARTHRITIS = 0;
    else ARTHRITIS = .;

    /* CHCCOPD3: ever told you have COPD or emphysema
       Confirmed values: 1 (Yes=2,093), 2 (No=22,798),
       7 (Don't know=98), 9 (Refused=10), 1 pre-missing          */
    if CHCCOPD3 = 1 then COPD = 1;
    else if CHCCOPD3 = 2 then COPD = 0;
    else COPD = .;

run;


/* Validation -- confirmed clean from Phase 1 output */
title 'Phase 1: Descriptive Statistics for Continuous Variables';
proc means data=brfss_clean n nmiss mean std min max;
    var PHYSHLTH_R MENTHLTH_R BMI AGE_C INCOME EDUCATION;
run;

title 'Phase 1: Frequency Counts for Categorical and Binary Variables';
proc freq data=brfss_clean;
    tables DEPRESSED EXERCISE SMOKER DIABETES_BIN
           BMI_CAT BMI_LABEL SDHBILLS_R TRANSPORT_BARRIER
           HEARTATTACK ARTHRITIS COPD / missing nocum;
run;
title;

Test 1: Hypothesis Testing (Two-Sample t-Test)

Objective:
The two-sample t-test will allow us to confirm whether or not adults who have been diagnosed with depression also experience significantly more poor mental health days per month when compared to those who have not been diagnosed. 

Variables: 
DEPRESSED (Grouped by this variable): Depression Diagnosis, 0 = No, 1= Yes 
- the depressed variable was recoded utilizing the variable ADDEPEV3

MENTHLTH_R (Outcome): This variable displays the number of days in the past month that a respondent reported poor mental health. It will be utilized to compare the average days of poor mental health between those diagnosed with Depression and those not. The variable is continuous, ranging from 0 to 30.

Hypothesis: 
H₀: The average number of days with poor mental health for individuals diagnosed will be equal to those who are not diagnosed. 
        mu_depressed = mu_not_derpessed
Hₐ: The average number of days with poor mental health for individuals diagnosed will not be equal to those who are not diagnosed. 
        mu_depressed ≠ mu_not_derpessed
SAS Code
title ' Two-Sample t-Test';
title2 'Mean Poor Mental Health Days by Depression Diagnosis';

proc ttest data=brfss_clean;
    class DEPRESSED;
    var MENTHLTH_R;
    where not missing(DEPRESSED) and not missing(MENTHLTH_R);
run;

title;
SAS Output

Two-Sample t-Test

Mean Poor Mental Health Days by Depression Diagnosis

The TTEST Procedure

 

Variable: MENTHLTH_R

DEPRESSED Method N Mean Std Dev Std Err Minimum Maximum
0   19247 2.5813 6.2608 0.0451 0 30.0000
1   5170 11.1975 11.0672 0.1539 0 30.0000
Diff (1-2) Pooled   -8.6162 7.5386 0.1181    
Diff (1-2) Satterthwaite   -8.6162   0.1604    
DEPRESSED Method Mean 95% CL Mean Std Dev 95% CL Std Dev
0   2.5813 2.4928 2.6697 6.2608 6.1989 6.3240
1   11.1975 10.8957 11.4992 11.0672 10.8579 11.2847
Diff (1-2) Pooled -8.6162 -8.8477 -8.3847 7.5386 7.4723 7.6060
Diff (1-2) Satterthwaite -8.6162 -8.9306 -8.3018      
Method Variances DF t Value Pr > |t|
Pooled Equal 24415 -72.96 <.0001
Satterthwaite Unequal 6083.8 -53.72 <.0001
Equality of Variances
Method Num DF Den DF F Value Pr > F
Folded F 5169 19246 3.12 <.0001

Interpretation of Results:

When analyzing the results from the following test, we are able to conclude that adults diagnosed with depression experience significantly more poor mental health on average per month compared to those without a diagnosis. The mean number of days for individuals without a diagnosis was shown to be approximately 2.58 days, while those with a diagnosis experienced an average of around 11.19 days. The F test indicated unequal variance, causing the Satterthwaite to be plied. With an 8.61-day difference, t(6083.8) = -53.72, a p < 0.0001, we have plausible evidence to reject our null hypothesis.

Test 2: Factor Analysis

Objective:
I plan to use factor analysis on 10 variables from the dataset that relate to behavioral outcomes, health outcomes, and chronic conditions. Our goal is to categorize these variables into meaningful groups.

Variables: 
HEARTATTACK: ever diagnosed with heart attack, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
ARTHRITIS: ever told you have arthritis, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
COPD: ever told you have COPD or emphysema, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
EXERCISE: exercised in past 30 days, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
BMI: body mass index, continuous
DIABETES_BIN: diabetes diagnosis, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
DEPRESSED: depression diagnosis, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
SMOKER: smoked 100+ cigarettes lifetime, 1 = Yes, 0 = No
MENTHLTH_R: poor mental health days, continuous 0-30
PHYSHLTH_R: poor physical health days, continuous 0-30
SAS Code
/* Factor Analysis Step 1: utilizing a correlation matrix to confirm variables are related */
title 'Phase 3 Step 1: Correlation Matrix';

proc corr data=brfss_clean;
    var PHYSHLTH_R MENTHLTH_R BMI DIABETES_BIN
        DEPRESSED SMOKER EXERCISE HEARTATTACK ARTHRITIS COPD;
    where not missing(PHYSHLTH_R) and not missing(MENTHLTH_R)
      and not missing(BMI) and not missing(DIABETES_BIN)
      and not missing(DEPRESSED) and not missing(SMOKER)
      and not missing(EXERCISE) and not missing(HEARTATTACK)
      and not missing(ARTHRITIS) and not missing(COPD);
run;
SAS Output

Phase 3 Step 1: Correlation Matrix

The CORR Procedure

1.00000
 

Results from the correlation Matrix: The results display that between the variables, there is a moderately strong correlation. From the results, we can note that one of the more notable relationships is between DEPRESSED and MENTHLTH_R. This indicates that those who are depressed tend to have an increased number of mental health days. Another notable relationship is between EXERCISE and PHYSHLTH_R, indicating that those who exercise in the past month tend to have fewer physical health days. Due to the mix of binary and continuous variables, no correlations exceeded 0.5, as binary variables tend to be limited in the size of correlations they produce. With these results, I feel comfortable continuing to the next stage of factor analysis.

SAS Code
/*The following will produce a scree plot that will allow us to determine how many factors to retain. We generally chose eigenvalues greater
than 1, but in the case the next value gets you close to the cummulative explaining 100% of the data we may retain that value as well */
title 'Determining the number of factors to retain';

proc factor data=brfss_clean
    method=principal
    plots=scree;
    var PHYSHLTH_R MENTHLTH_R BMI DIABETES_BIN
        DEPRESSED SMOKER EXERCISE HEARTATTACK ARTHRITIS COPD;
    where not missing(PHYSHLTH_R) and not missing(MENTHLTH_R)
      and not missing(BMI) and not missing(DIABETES_BIN)
      and not missing(DEPRESSED) and not missing(SMOKER)
      and not missing(EXERCISE) and not missing(HEARTATTACK)
      and not missing(ARTHRITIS) and not missing(COPD);
run;
SAS Output

Determining the number of factors to retain

The FACTOR Procedure

Input Data Type Raw Data
Number of Records Read 20081
Number of Records Used 20081
N for Significance Tests 20081

Determining the number of factors to retain

The FACTOR Procedure

Initial Factor Method: Principal Components

Prior Communality Estimates: ONE

Eigenvalues of the Correlation Matrix: Total = 10 Average = 1
  Eigenvalue Difference Proportion Cumulative

3 factors will be retained by the MINEIGEN criterion.

Factor Pattern
  Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
PHYSHLTH_R 0.65625 -0.09278 -0.00093

Factor1 Factor2 Factor3
2.2106510 1.2931669 1.1045022
Final Communality Estimates: Total = 4.608320
PHYSHLTH_R MENTHLTH_R BMI DIABETES_BIN DEPRESSED SMOKER EXERCISE HEARTATTACK ARTHRITIS COPD
0.43927747 0.69724583 0.57450375 0.49206106 0.60053390 0.41034259 0.28300092 0.32310817 0.34969507 0.43855135

Scree Plot Results Analysis: Based off the results with confidence I plan to retain 3 factors as those factors will cummulatively account for ~46% of the variance while containing eigenvalues that are all greater than our 1 threshold.

SAS Code
/*The following will complete out factor analysis while retaining the three factors as we chose */
title 'Phase 3 Step 3: Rotated Factor Solution';
title2 'Update nfactors= based on scree plot before running';

proc factor data=brfss_clean
    method=principal
    nfactors=3          /* UPDATE THIS after reviewing scree plot */
    rotate=varimax;
    var PHYSHLTH_R MENTHLTH_R BMI DIABETES_BIN
        DEPRESSED SMOKER EXERCISE HEARTATTACK ARTHRITIS COPD;
    where not missing(PHYSHLTH_R) and not missing(MENTHLTH_R)
      and not missing(BMI) and not missing(DIABETES_BIN)
      and not missing(DEPRESSED) and not missing(SMOKER)
      and not missing(EXERCISE) and not missing(HEARTATTACK)
      and not missing(ARTHRITIS) and not missing(COPD);
run;

title;

0.43927747 0.69724583 0.57450375 0.49206106 0.60053390 0.41034259 0.28300092 0.32310817 0.34969507 0.43855135

Factor Analysis Resutls Takeaways:

Based on our results, we are able to state that seven of the variables loaded onto three different factors, with Factor 1 being what could be Mental Health (MENTHLTH_R, DEPRESSED), Factor 2 being Chronic Diseases (SMOKER, HEARTATTACK, COPD), and Factor 3  being Metabolic Risk (BMI, DIABETES_BIN). The variables that did not exceed 0.5 indicate that they do not clearly belong in a factor were PHYSHLTH_R, ARTHRITIS, and EXERCISE.

Test 3: Multiple Linear Regression

Objective: Utilizing multiple linear regression, I hope to determine which behavioral and demographic factors are significant independent predictors of poor physical health days per month. This is done while also ensuring that all other predictors are controlled simultaneously.

Variables: • PHYSHLTH_R (outcome): poor physical health days per month, continuous 0-30 • BMI (predictor): body mass index, continuous • AGE_C (predictor): imputed age from _AGE80, continuous (capped at 80) • SEX (predictor): 0 = Male, 1 = Female • SMOKER (predictor): 0 = Non-smoker, 1 = Smoker • EXERCISE (predictor): 0 = No exercise, 1 = Exercises • INCOME (predictor): income level, ordinal 1-11 • EDUCATION (predictor): education level, ordinal 1-6

Hypothesis: H₀: None of the following variables have a significant linear relationship with PHYSHLTH_R and all true coefficients are equal to 0

Hₐ: At least one of the following variables does have a significant linear relationship with PHYSHLTH_R (Has a non-zero coefficient)
SAS Code
/*The following creates a clean subset of the data where all
missing values in all variables have been removed*/


data brfss_reg;
    set brfss_clean;
    where not missing(PHYSHLTH_R) and not missing(BMI)
      and not missing(AGE_C) and not missing(SEX)
      and not missing(SMOKER) and not missing(EXERCISE)
      and not missing(INCOME) and not missing(EDUCATION);
run;
SAS Code
title 'A Multiple Linear Regression Utilizing Backward Selection';
title2 'Outcome: Poor Physical Health Days (PHYSHLTH_R)';

proc glmselect data=brfss_reg
    outdesign(addinputvars)=Work.reg_design
    plots=(criterionpanel);
    model PHYSHLTH_R = BMI AGE_C SEX SMOKER EXERCISE INCOME EDUCATION
        / showpvalues
          selection=backward
          (slstay=0.05 select=sl stop=sl)
          details=steps;
run;

title ' Regression Diagnostics';
proc reg data=Work.reg_design alpha=0.05
    plots(only)=(diagnostics residuals observedbypredicted);
    ods select DiagnosticsPanel ResidualPlot ObservedByPredicted;
    model PHYSHLTH_R = &_GLSMOD /;
run;
quit;

title;
SAS Output

Outcome: Poor Physical Health Days (PHYSHLTH_R)

Regression Diagnostics

The REG Procedure

Model: MODEL1

Dependent Variable: PHYSHLTH_R

Results Explained from Multiple Linear Regression Test:

Upon analyzing the residual plots, I concluded that the linearity assumption was supported, as the residuals for all six variables were centered around zero. The independence assumption was satisfied due to the sample design of our dataset. However, there is a mild concern regarding equal variance, as both the BMI and Income plots exhibit a slight fan shape. Additionally, I was advised (By Claude) that the normality assumption showed slight skewness, indicated by a hard floor around -10 for our plots and a long upper tail. This is a known limitation associated with bounded outcome variables.

Following the test the model rataint 6 predictive variabels (BMI, AGE_C, SEX, SMOKER, EXERCISE, INCOME) removing Education the model. Went through loops removing variables by P value. The results from our model seem to be quite strong with a high F value of 364.46 and a moderately strong R^2 of 0.1069 (indicating that it explains ~10.7% of the variance in PHYSHLTH_R)

Test 4: One-Way ANOVA

Objective: The reason for utilizing an ANOVA is that we hope to see whether or not poor physical health days significantly change when comparing the four BMI categories.

Variables: PHYSHLTH_R (outcome): poor physical health days per month, continuous 0-30 BMI_LABEL (grouping factor): four levels -- Underweight, Normal, Overweight, Obese

Hypothesis: H₀: That across all four BMI categories the mean number of poor phyiscal health days are equal. mu_underweight = mu_normal = mu_overweight = mu_obese 

Hₐ: That at least one of the BMI categories has a significantly different mean number of poor health days.
SAS Code
/*The following produces a new subset dataset without missing valuee*/
data brfss_anova;
    set brfss_clean;
    where not missing(PHYSHLTH_R) and not missing(BMI_LABEL);
run;

title ' One-Way ANOVA';
title2 'Poor Physical Health Days by BMI Category';

proc glm data=brfss_anova;
    class BMI_LABEL;
    model PHYSHLTH_R = BMI_LABEL;
    means BMI_LABEL / hovtest=levene tukey cldiff;
run;
quit;

title;
SAS Output

One-Way ANOVA

Poor Physical Health Days by BMI Category

The GLM Procedure

BMI_LABEL 4 Normal Obese Overweight Underweight

Poor Physical Health Days by BMI Category

The GLM Procedure

 

Tukey's Studentized Range (HSD) Test for PHYSHLTH_R

Note:This test controls the Type I experimentwise error rate.

Alpha 0.05
Error Degrees of Freedom 22100
Error Mean Square 79.30766
Critical Value of Studentized Range 3.63342
Comparisons significant at the 0.05 level are indicated by ***.
BMI_LABEL
Comparison		 Difference
Between
Means		 Simultaneous 95% Confidence Limits  
Underweight - Obese 0.2957 -0.8843 1.4757  
Underweight - Normal 2.0066 0.8222 3.1909 ***
Underweight - Overweight 2.0336 0.8549 3.2122 ***
Obese - Underweight -0.2957 -1.4757 0.8843  
Obese - Normal 1.7108 1.3220 2.0997 ***
Obese - Overweight 1.7378 1.3669 2.1088 ***
Normal - Underweight -2.0066 -3.1909 -0.8222 ***
Normal - Obese -1.7108 -2.0997 -1.3220 ***
Normal - Overweight 0.0270 -0.3576 0.4116  
Overweight - Underweight -2.0336 -3.2122 -0.8549 ***
Overweight - Obese -1.7378 -2.1088 -1.3669 ***
Overweight - Normal -0.0270 -0.4116 0.3576  

One-Way ANOVA Results:

Upon analyzing our output, we confidently conclude that there is a significant statistical difference in the mean number of poor physical health days across the different BMI categories. This is supported by a high F-value of 64.52 and a p-value of less than 0.0001. However, our R-squared value of approximately 0.0087 indicates that BMI category alone explains less than 1% of the variance in PHYSHLTH_R, suggesting that BMI is not a strong standalone predictor.

A potential concern emerged during our ANOVA regarding Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance for PHYSHLTH_R, where our p-value was reported as less than 0.0001. This result indicates that the four groups do not possess equal variance.

Interestingly, the boxplot illustrating the distribution of PHYSHLTH_R revealed that the underweight category has the highest median number of poor health days, followed by the obese category. Additionally, it is worth noting that the median number of poor health days for the normal and overweight categories is remarkably similar.

Tukey's Studentized Range Test indicated that significant differences in means were present among the groups of underweight-obese, underweight-normal, obese-normal, obese-underweight, again underweight-obese, and overweight-obese. In contrast, the groups underweight-obese and normal-overweight did not exhibit statistical significance. This suggests that respondents in these groups tended to report similar health outcomes.

Test 5: Logistic Regression

Objective: With the following test, I hope to identify the behavioral and demographic factors that significantly predict the chances of having a diabetes diagnosis.

Variables: DIABETES_BIN (binary outcome): diabetes diagnosis, 1 = Yes, 0 = No BMI (predictor): body mass index, continuous AGE_C (predictor): imputed age from _AGE80, continuous (capped at 80) SEX (predictor): 0 = Male (reference), 1 = Female SMOKER (predictor): 0 = Non-smoker (reference), 1 = Smoker EXERCISE (predictor): 1 = Exercises (reference), 0 = No exercise INCOME (predictor): income level, ordinal 1-11

Hypothesis: H₀: None of the predictor variables is effective in predicting the chances of a diabetes diagnosis. With this, all population slope coefficients are equal to zero.     Hₐ: That at least one of the predictor variables can significantly predict the chances of a diabetes diagnosis.

SAS Code

data brfss_logistic;
    set brfss_clean;
    where not missing(DIABETES_BIN) and not missing(BMI)
      and not missing(AGE_C) and not missing(SEX)
      and not missing(SMOKER) and not missing(EXERCISE)
      and not missing(INCOME);
run;

title 'Logistic Regression';
title2 'Predictors of Diabetes Diagnosis';

proc logistic data=brfss_logistic;
    class SEX     (ref='0')    /* Reference: Male           */
          SMOKER  (ref='0')    /* Reference: Non-smoker     */
          EXERCISE(ref='1')    /* Reference: Exercises      */
          / param=ref;
    model DIABETES_BIN(event='1') = BMI AGE_C SEX SMOKER EXERCISE INCOME
        / selection=backward
          slstay=0.05;
    oddsratio BMI    / cl=wald;
    oddsratio AGE_C  / cl=wald;
    oddsratio INCOME / cl=wald;
run;
SAS Output

Logistic Regression

Predictors of Diabetes Diagnosis

Error		 Wald

AGE_C   1 0.0466 0.00157 880.8173 <.0001
SEX 1 1 -0.3407 0.0452 56.7859 <.0001

EXERCISE 0 1 0.3981 0.0491 65.7049 <.0001

BMI 1.086 1.079 1.093
AGE_C 1.048 1.044 1.051

EXERCISE 0 vs 1 1.489 1.352 1.639

Percent Tied 0.0 Tau-a 0.137
Pairs 42026592 c 0.764
Odds Ratio Estimates and Wald Confidence Intervals
Odds Ratio Estimate 95% Confidence Limits
BMI 1.086 1.079 1.093
AGE_C 1.048 1.044 1.051
INCOME 0.911 0.894 0.928

Logistic Regression Results:

The response profile of our dataset comprised of 15,216 individuals not diagnosed with diabetes and 2,762 individuals with the condition. Six variables underwent backward selection, and all were retained as their P-values consistently remained below 0.05. The model was deemed significant, as evidenced by a p-value of less than 0.0001 in the global null hypothesis test.

Our Odds Ratio Estimates displayed that: BMI: for everyone 1 unit higher you are 8.6% more likley to have diabetes AGE_C: For every year older you are 4.8% more likely Sex 1 vs 0: Females are ~29% less likely then males Smoker 1 vs 0: Those who smoke are 11% more likely Exercise 0 vs 1: Those who did not exercise are ~49% more likely Income: The higher the income, you are 9% less likely

The odds ratio estimates unsuprisingly showed that those who do not exercise are ~49% more likely. This was the strongest predictor.

The model yielded a C-statistic of 0.764, indicating acceptable accuracy, as it exceeds the typical threshold of 0.70. Consequently, the model successfully distinguished between diabetic and non-diabetic respondents 76% of the time.

Project Conclusion:

The following project analyzed data from 25,000 respondents in the BRFSS dataset and consistently found that physical inactivity, smoking, increased BMI, older age, and lower income were the strongest predictors of poor health outcomes. The factor analysis grouped the variables into three effective categories: mental health, chronic disease risk, and metabolic risk. The regression model explained approximately 10.7% of the variance in poor physical health days, with our project determining that the exercise variable was the strongest predictor across both the linear and logistic models. The logistic regression further reinforced this conclusion. A consistent theme highlighted throughout all tests was that lifestyle and socioeconomic factors, specifically physical activity and income, are the most significant drivers of health outcomes within the population.

Stonehill College  ·  Multivariate Statistics Directed Study  ·  Spring 2026
